bibliographic_data.title: Low Ambiguity in Strong, Total, Associative, One-Way Functions
bibliographic_data.abstract: Rabi and Sherman [RS97] present a cryptographic paradigm based on associative, one-way functions that are strong (i.e., hard to invert even if one of their arguments is given) and total. Hemaspaandra and Rothe [HR99] proved that such powerful one-way functions exist exactly if (standard) one-way functions exist, thus showing that the associative one-way function approach is as plausible as previous approaches. In the present paper, we study the degree of ambiguity of one-way functions. Rabi and Sherman showed that no associative one-way function (over a universe having at least two elements) can be unambiguous (i.e., one-to-one). Nonetheless, we prove that if standard, unambiguous, one-way functions exist, then there exist strong, total, associative, one-way functions that are O(n)-to-one. This puts a reasonable upper bound on the ambiguity. Our other main results are: 1. P ≠ FewP if and only if there exists an (nO(1))-to-one, strong, total AOWF. 2. No O(1)-to-one total, associative functions exist in Σ∗×Σ∗→Σ∗. 3. For every nondecreasing, unbounded, total, recursive function g : N →N, there is a g(n)-to-one, total, commutative, associative, recursive function in Σ∗×Σ∗→Σ∗.

core_content.novelty_claim: The paper proves that if standard unambiguous one-way functions exist, then there exist strong, total, associative, one-way functions that are O(n)-to-one, thereby putting a reasonable upper bound on the ambiguity.
core_content.key_methodology: The authors study the degree of ambiguity in one-way functions and prove several results related to their existence under different conditions.
outcomes: Proved that no total, associative function in Σ∗×Σ∗→Σ∗ is O(1)-to-one.
Showed that if standard unambiguous one-way functions exist, then there exist strong, total, associative, one-way functions that are O(n)-to-one.
Established a lower bound on the ambiguity of certain classes of total, associative functions.
core_content.full_text_markdown: The paper explores the concept of ambiguity in one-way functions. Rabi and Sherman showed that no associative one-way function over a universe with at least two elements can be unambiguous (one-to-one). However, this paper proves that if standard unambiguous one-way functions exist, then there are strong, total, associative, one-way functions that are O(n)-to-one, thus providing an upper bound on the ambiguity. The authors also prove that P ≠ FewP if and only if nO(1)-to-one, strong, total, associative one-way functions exist. Additionally, they establish a lower bound on the ambiguity of certain classes of total, associative functions.
